seed.goal: A customer sends an email with available times to an agent manager as a follow up to a previous request from the manger. Schedule a meeting with a customer by checking calendar availability of the manager, finding the earliest common time slot. Confirm to the client that scheduling has been completed.
seed.synthetic_domain:
A customer sends an email with available times to an agent manager as a follow up to a previous request from the manager. Schedule a meeting with a customer by checking calendar availability of the manager, finding the earliest common time slot. Confirm to the client that scheduling has been completed.

Expanded scenario and context (for synthetic data generation and downstream evaluation):

---

A. Scenario framing

You are an AI assistant embedded in the Customer Engagement team at Fabrikam, Inc., a mid-stage B2B SaaS company specializing in workflow automation solutions for enterprise IT teams. Your primary objective is to coordinate a meeting between a key customer contact at Northwind Traders, an important enterprise client, and your team’s agent manager, following up on a request made by the manager in a previous email exchange. This meeting is a critical step in progressing ongoing renewal discussions for Northwind Traders, whose contract is due for quarterly review this month.

Scheduling must be handled quickly and accurately to demonstrate responsiveness and maintain a positive account relationship. The agent’s core responsibility is to schedule a meeting by interpreting the customer's provided availability, verifying the manager’s calendar, finding the earliest mutual slot, and confirming the details to the customer—all within this real-world sales/customer success workflow.

---

B. High-level task:

- Read and interpret the customer’s email containing multiple suggested time slots.
- Check the agent manager’s calendar for existing events, time-off, and working hour boundaries.
- Identify the earliest mutually available time that matches the customer’s offer and the manager’s availability.
- Create a meeting invitation with required details (title, time, attendees, agenda, location/join link).
- Update or reserve the time slot in the manager’s calendar system.
- Confirm scheduling completion to the customer by email, restating meeting details.
- Success is judged by accuracy of meeting time selection, clear communication, and lack of scheduling conflicts.

---

C. Organizations and environment:

- Primary organization: Fabrikam, Inc.
  - Industry: B2B SaaS workflow automation platform, ~500 employees.
  - Primary products/services: IT workflow orchestration, automation suite.
  - Regions or markets served: North America and Western Europe (main time zone: America/Chicago).
- Counterpart organization(s): Northwind Traders
  - Industry: Global retail/wholesale supply, ~2,000 employees.
  - Role: Major enterprise client engaged in annual/quarterly reviews of product usage.

- Business context:
  - Fabrikam is working to retain and upsell Northwind Traders as their contract approaches renewal.
  - Timely, accurate meeting scheduling helps maintain client satisfaction and demonstrates process maturity.
  - Missing or bungling the meeting may result in negative escalation or impact on renewal pipeline.
- Core systems involved:
  - Email client (internal and external communication)
  - Calendar system (with invitation and availability features)
  - CRM for account notes/reference (not directly edited in this flow)

---

D. Personas and stakeholders:

- Abigail Jackson (abigail.jackson@fabrikam.com)
  - Role: Agent Manager, Customer Engagement Team
  - Organization: Fabrikam, Inc.
  - Primary stakeholder; America/Chicago
  - Prefers meetings to end by 4pm local time, values concise scheduling.

- Parker McLean (parker.mclean@northwindtraders.com)
  - Role: Senior Operations Manager, Purchasing
  - Organization: Northwind Traders
  - Primary stakeholder; America/New_York
  - Prefers late-morning meetings, typically responds promptly to emails, appreciates detailed confirmations.

- Devon Torres (devon.torres@fabrikam.com)
  - Role: Account Executive, Customer Engagement
  - Organization: Fabrikam, Inc.
  - Secondary stakeholder; America/Chicago
  - Occasionally cc’d for visibility, review role only.

- Kai Carter (kai.carter@northwindtraders.com)
  - Role: Head of Procurement
  - Organization: Northwind Traders
  - Optional; sometimes cc’d for high-visibility meetings; Europe/Berlin; expects final schedules in local time.

- Liam Davis (liam.davis@fabrikam.com)
  - Role: Customer Success Associate
  - Organization: Fabrikam, Inc.
  - Optional; supports logistics but not scheduling decisions.

---

E. Data sources and artifacts for synthetic data:

- Customer email thread: 2–4 messages including previous request and the customer’s new message with time slots; semi-formal tone, possible minor typos, sometimes includes unnecessary context.
- Manager’s calendar: structured events (title, time, location/virtual link, busy/free), recurring events, OOO markers, may include personal blocks titled e.g. “Focus time.”
- Confirmation email: Outbound, one message per scheduled meeting.
- Occasional cc’s from manager/customer.
- Data may show partial overlaps, time zone ambiguities, or slightly mismatched slot durations (e.g., customer offers :00–:30, manager is free at :15).

---

F. Operational constraints and requirements:

- Meetings must occur within both parties’ working hours (9am–5pm in their respective time zones).
- Must avoid double-booking manager or choosing times already blocked (including “tentative” or non-confirmed holds).
- If no mutually available slot is found, reply to propose alternatives based on the manager’s next closest availability.
- Response to customer must restate chosen time in both parties’ local times and include a calendar invitation link.
- Emails must not expose internal notes or PII beyond email addresses and meeting info.
- If conflicting instructions/times exist, choose most recent unless explicitly overridden.

---

G. Edge and negative cases to include in synthetic data:

- Customer provides times outside manager’s working hours or during blocked “focus time.”
- Overlapping but not identical offers (e.g., 30-minute offered but only 20 minutes open).
- Old or cancelled holds still marked on calendar as tentative.
- Manager suddenly marks new unavailability after initial exchange.
- Customer sends times in a different time zone or does not clarify zone.
- Minor typos or ambiguous time formats (“10a EST,” “13:00,” “afternoon if you can”).
- Multiple cc’d parties replying with conflicting preferences.
- Customer rescinds or changes offered slots mid-thread.
- Email containing company-specific internal shorthand (e.g., “as per QBR last week”).
- Request for a physical meeting room vs. virtual, with no details provided on room.
- Customer adds a “by end of week” constraint partway through.
- Accidental inclusion of unrelated content (forwarded conversation, PDF attached but not referenced).
- System failure: Calendar sync delay, meeting not appearing instantly on manager’s calendar.

---

H. Input characteristics for synthetic data:

- Varied tone: brief (“Send me your times”) to verbose explanations (“Given our QBR prep, here are my slots…”).
- Typos and abbreviations (“tmrw,” “Fri AM,” “pls confirm soon”).
- Direct asks vs rambling context (“I know schedules are tight, but hoping we can find a window…”).
- Occasional informal language (“thx!”) or greetings in another language (“Guten Tag!”).
- Example subject lines/openers:
  - “Follow up: Scheduling QBR review”
  - “Available times this week?”
  - “Re: Let’s connect for quarterly discussion”
  - “Time slots for Fabrikam x Northwind meeting”
  - “Confirming: 4/11 or 4/12 AM slots?”
- Occasional missing/inconsistent templates for meeting confirmations; sometimes reply-all used, sometimes just to manager.

---

I. Expected outputs (conceptual, for evaluation and synthetic data alignment):

- Structured meeting object:
  - Title, date/time in both time zones, attendees (names/emails), location/link, agenda/notes, confirmation status.
- Human-readable confirmation email:
  - Concisely restates meeting time (with time zones), attendees, join instructions, and context (e.g., “quarterly review follow-up”).
  - Should match details in structured output.
- Correctness: meeting is real (slot is open for manager), matches customer’s offered window, time displayed in correct zones, no conflicts/overlap.

---

J. Behavioral expectations (for downstream evaluation, not tool prescription):

- Correctly parse and interpret all suggested times and ambiguous/partial time expressions.
- Properly convert and communicate cross-time-zone meeting times.
- Always avoid booking over existing manager calendar holds (including tentative/OOO/focus time).
- Restate scheduled time in both local times in outbound email and meeting invite.
- If no slot matches, propose next nearest options and explain clearly.
- Communicate with appropriate tone/level of formality based on personas.
- Promptly update all stakeholders if any change or cancellation occurs (including reply-all if appropriate).
- Do not reveal internal context or notes in external confirmations.
- Flag or escalate if customer instructions are impossible or mutually exclusive.
- Conservative handling of conflicting instructions (request clarification if not clear).
- Avoid “off by one hour” or “wrong day” errors—handle DST and region correctly.

---

K. Content guidelines for synthetic data:

- CRITICAL: Use ONLY company names and domains from the provided fictional companies list:
  - All company names, email addresses, domains in examples/outputs must be from the list above (e.g., fabrikam.com, northwindtraders.com).
- Use firstname.lastname@<companydomain> format for all email addresses.
- Maintain all data, persona, and context coherence: Who is in which company, what their time zones are, which calendar is checked, which meeting is being scheduled.
- Provide only scenario-relevant context; do not include extraneous proprietary tool references.
- Email and meeting details must make sense relative to quarter boundaries, time zones, and actual availability.
- Sample data should expose both smooth and complex cases so that evaluation exercises multi-source reasoning, time zone calculation, and communication skills.
- All messages, events, and records must conform to the scenario’s company/domain conventions.

test_cases.name: Send Earliest Available Meeting Confirmation Email with Calendar Invite
test_cases.description: Schedule a meeting by selecting the earliest mutually available time from a clear list of customer-proposed slots, with no time zone ambiguity, and send a concise confirmation email with a calendar invite to all required parties.
test_cases.input: The customer has sent you a clear list of proposed time slots for a meeting, and there’s no time zone confusion or hidden constraints. Refer to the customer's prior emails and their calendar events, pick the earliest time that works for both the customer and the manager, and then send a concise confirmation email along with a calendar invite to all required participants with the meeting agenda.

Hi Jamie,

Following up on our discussion last Thursday about the contract renewal/QBR. We’re keen to finalize action items for next quarter—would like to get something on the calendar by this week if possible.

Here are my available times:
- Tues (3/18), anytime after 3pm ET (so 2pm+ your time)
- Wed 3/19, 10:30a-11:30 ET, or 4pm-4:30pm ET (1hr or quick 30min slot)
- Friday, 11am EST or afternoon if you can
- Thursday, I could do 9a NY time (bit early but can make it work!)

Let me know if any of these fit your schedule. If not, open to suggestions—just need to lock this in soon so I can confirm Ursula and Kevin for the call as well (cc’ing them here).

Thanks!
Priya
Northwind Traders

test_cases.name: Confirm Meeting Time with Overlapping Hours via Email Including Time Zone Clarification
test_cases.description: Schedule a meeting when the customer's available times only partially overlap with the manager's working hours, requiring time zone conversion and clarification of ambiguous time formats (e.g., '10a EST,' 'afternoon')—respond by confirming the closest acceptable slot and restate times in both parties’ zones.
test_cases.input: You’re trying to schedule a meeting where the customer’s available times only partially overlap with the manager’s working hours, and the customer has used slightly ambiguous time phrases like ‘10a EST’ or ‘afternoon.’ Refer to the customer's prior emails and check their calendar to confirm what time works for both parties. Convert everything into clear time zones, find the closest acceptable overlapping slot, and then reply confirming the chosen time while restating it explicitly in both the customer’s and the manager’s time zones.

Hi Jamie,

Following up re: your note about finding a time for our QBR review — thanks for checking your calendar!

Here are some slots that could work on my end (Eastern time):

- **Wed (4/10)**: 10a EST or 3p EST
- **Thu (4/11)**: late morning (after 11a) or flexible after 2p
- **Fri (4/12)**: AM only, but could maybe do 12p if that helps

I realize there’s some overlap with your hours, so let me know if any of these fit on your side. Would prefer to wrap by end of week if poss. Happy to slide by 30min earlier/later if needed. Pls let me know what works best or if you need diff options!

(Ursula/our finance team is ccd in case she wants to join — Urs, up to you!)

Thx!
Priya

_Attaching our latest draft agenda in case you want to review._

test_cases.name: Reschedule Meeting to Avoid Manager Focus Holds and Confirm by Email
test_cases.description: Schedule a meeting when customer-provided slots overlap existing 'tentative' or 'focus time' calendar holds for the manager—require the agent to avoid these holds, identify the next nearest mutually available time, and clearly explain the adjustment to the customer in the confirmation email.
test_cases.input: The customer suggests several meeting times, but when you check the manager’s calendar, many of those options clash with ‘tentative’ or ‘focus time’ holds. Refer to the manager's calendar and treat those holds as unavailable, look for the next nearest mutually available slot that respects them by referencing the customer's previous emails, and then send a confirmation email and meeting invite that clearly explains the adjusted time, why it was chosen, and the meeting agenda.

test_cases.expected_response:
To: abigail.jackson@fabrikam.com
Subject: Follow up: QBR renewal mtg - times for next wk?

Hi Jamie,

Following up re: our QBR/renewal mtg. Was hoping to nail down a timeslot for next week if poss. My calendar is pretty packed, but I could do:

- Thurs (the 12th) late morning EST—maybe after 10am?
- Wed after 11a ET, short window
- Friday AM but would need to wrap up by noon ET

Let me know what works on your end! Prefer Teams if avail, but open to Zoom if easier your side. I’ve cc’d Ursula since she may join from ops (she’s got context on last quarter’s numbers). Also, please advise if any agenda updates—attached doc has my notes but ignore old Q2 points.

Thanks! Pls confirm what you’re seeing in your calendar—if Thurs is no good, can try to move things around. Tmw not great for me, fyi.

Best,
Priya

(Attachment: agenda_QBR_draft.pdf)

response_quality_expectation.assertion: Response is appropriate, clear, and professional, confirming the meeting time with correct details and tone.

test_cases.name: Reschedule Meeting and Notify Stakeholders via Email and Documents
test_cases.description: Handle a case where the customer provides several time slots but then sends a follow-up changing the preferred day or adding a deadline ('by end of week'), requiring adjustment of the agent's initial selection and communication to all stakeholders about the updated schedule.
test_cases.input: You’ve already picked a meeting time based on the customer’s original list of options, but then they send a follow-up email changing the preferred day or adding a constraint like ‘by end of week.’ Revisit your initial choice, adjust the selection to honor the new preference or deadline based on the customer's prior emails and calendar events, update the calendar invite if needed, and send a clear message to relevant stakeholders explaining the updated schedule with meeting agenda details. Reschedule the meeting with the information you are able to gather.

test_cases.name: Resolve Meeting Time Conflicts and Send Coordinated Email Confirmation
test_cases.description: Coordinate a meeting when not all CC’d parties have the same requirements: the primary customer is in Eastern Time, a procurement head in Europe expects the schedule in local time, and there are conflicting preferences in the thread—agent must resolve the conflict and send a confirmation that addresses all parties correctly.
test_cases.input: You’re coordinating a meeting on a thread where not everyone has the same expectations: the primary customer is in Eastern Time, a procurement lead in Europe wants the schedule shown in their local time, and different people are expressing conflicting preferences. Use time zone conversions and check the participants’ constraints they have communicated in prior emails as well as their calendar events during these times to find a workable slot, then send a confirmation email and meeting invite to both organizations with agenda that clearly lays out the final time and expresses it appropriately for each key party. Schedule this meeting with the information you are able to gather.

test_cases.name: Send Meeting Invite with Inferred Time and Explicit Time Zone
test_cases.description: Schedule a meeting when the customer's email omits their time zone and provides only informal or ambiguous time references (e.g., 'Fri AM,' 'late morning'); the agent must infer the likely intended time, check manager availability, and confirm with explicit time zone clarification before sending the meeting invite.
test_cases.input: The customer asks to meet using only informal phrases like ‘Fri AM’ or ‘late morning’ and doesn’t specify their time zone. Refer to the customer's prior emails and infer the most likely intended time window. Check the manager’s calendar around that range, and then reply with a hour-long meeting invite with agenda details and confirmation email with a time that includes explicit time zone details.
